title: Rendering Formal SignWriting
subtitle: SVG, fonts, layout, and output models
summary: Canonical statement of SVG, fonts, layout, and output models kept distinct from canonical text.
synopsis: Formal SignWriting needs a clear distinction between canonical text and presentation layer. This entry states the rendering layer in its smallest stable form and explains SVG, fonts, standalone graphics, and web layout as rendering consequences of the model, while keeping styling as a separate presentation topic.

slides.title: Rendering Is Downstream
slides.summary: Rendering is a technical consequence of the model rather than the model itself.

image_prompts.concrete: Technical rendering slide showing encoded text becoming SVG and browser output, clean academic software style, white background
image_prompts.abstract: Structured sign text resolving into visible graphical form

slides.speech: This slide separates canonical text from presentation. Formal SignWriting can be rendered and displayed in multiple ways, but those outputs remain downstream from the underlying text model. Styling matters here, but its detailed definition belongs in its own layer rather than being confused with the encoding itself. Rendering is the visible consequence of the model, not the definition of the model.
slides.notes: Keep the distinction between text and rendering very explicit.

slides.title: Multiple Output Paths, One Source Text
slides.summary: Rendering needs practical output models that preserve copyable text and visible form together.

image_prompts.concrete: Rendering architecture slide showing one Formal SignWriting source producing font based SVG, standalone SVG, and PNG outputs, elegant technical style
image_prompts.abstract: One source text unfolding into several visible output formats

slides.speech: Formal SignWriting rendering is not a single mechanism. It includes font-based SVG, stand-alone SVG assembled from graphic resources, and derivative bitmap output. The key architectural point is that these are all realizations of the text model rather than replacements for it. The source text stays stable while the output path can vary.
slides.notes: Show multiple output paths without losing the core distinction.

slides.title: Layout Is Where Rendering Gets Real
slides.summary: Rendering has to solve layout questions that arise from vertical writing, sign sizing, and lane alignment.

image_prompts.concrete: Web layout slide showing vertical columns of SignWriting with lanes, centering, and responsive page behavior, clean publication-quality design
image_prompts.abstract: Vertical sign text finding order and balance on a page

slides.speech: Rendering becomes interesting when signs move from isolated examples into real text layout. Lane-aware vertical flow, centering, sizing, and web layout are all part of how the model becomes usable in actual interfaces and documents. This is where the rendering layer proves that it can scale from one sign to a readable passage.
slides.notes: Tie rendering to real page behavior, not only isolated SVG.

slides.title: Rendering Needs a Boundary
slides.summary: A strong rendering architecture preserves canonical text while allowing multiple output strategies.
bullets: Do not confuse rendering with encoding
Do not confuse rendering with styling
Keep outputs downstream from the model
slides.speech: Rendering becomes cleaner when its boundary is explicit. It should realize the text model, not redefine it. It should work with styling, not collapse into styling. And it should keep the encoded text durable even when the visible output changes across platforms and generations. That boundary is what lets the rendering layer stay flexible without making the model unstable.
